q: Acid-base reactions are essential in the fields of chemistry such as ___, agricultural chemistry, industrial chemistry, and environmental chemistry.
When an acid and a base react, they ___ each other to form a salt.
Antacids such as ___ and magnesium hydroxide (magnesia) are taken when a person has a stomach ulcer.
Calcium oxide (quicklime) is applied to ___ to neutralise it.
___ can be used to absorb harmful sulphur dioxide released from the power station.
___ is a measure of the acidity or alkalinity of the solution.
Buffering in the ___ is crucial to our survival.
The first person to define acids and bases was the Swedish chemist ___.
Acids are any substances that ___ to produce hydrogen ions ($H^{+}$) when they dissolve in water.
___ such as hydrochloric acid completely ionise in water.
___ such as ethanoic acid only partially ionise.
Bases are any substances that dissociate to produce ___ when they dissolve in water.
An example of an Arrhenius base is ___.
Arrhenius theory is unable to account for the ___ of $CO_{2}$, $SO_{2}$, and $SO_{3}$.
Arrhenius theory is unable to account for the ___ of $CaO$ and $MgO$.
The Danish chemist Johannes Nicolaus Brønsted and the English chemist Thomas Martin Lowry independently proposed the ___.
According to Brønsted-Lowry, acids are ___ and bases are proton acceptors.
An extension of the Brønsted-Lowry definition of acids and bases is the concept of the ___.
The ___ of a Brønsted-Lowry acid is the species that remains when one proton has been removed from the acid.
A conjugate acid results from the ___ to a Brønsted-Lowry base.
Every Brønsted-Lowry acid and base has a corresponding ___.
A strong acid has a ___ conjugate base.
The ethanoic acid molecule donates a proton to water forming the ___ ion.
The $H_{2}O$ molecule accepts a proton forming the $H_{3}O^{+}$ ion, known as the ___ ion.
Substances such as $BF_{3}$ and $AlCl_{3}$ do not have any hydrogen and thus cannot give a proton but are known to behave as ___.
In 1932, American chemist ___ proposed that bases are species that can donate an electron pair.
Acids are species that can ___ an electron pair.
The $NH_{3}$ molecule behaves as a ___ because it possesses a lone pair of electrons.
The reaction of ammonia with ___ is an acid-base reaction forming a Lewis salt.
Water can dissociate into hydrogen and hydroxide ions, and the process is known as ___.
The number of hydrogen ions that water will receive determines the ___.
In reactions with acids like $HCl$, water acts as a ___.
In reactions with bases like $NH_{3}$, water acts as an ___.
Water is called an ___ molecule because it has both proton-accepting and proton-donating properties.
Pure water undergoes ionisation to a ___.
The equilibrium constant $K_{w} = [H^{+}][OH^{-}]$ is called the ___.
Since the process of autoionisation is ___, the concentrations of hydrogen ions and hydroxide ions increase with temperature.
If the individual concentration of hydrogen ions and hydroxide ions in an aqueous solution is equal to $1.0 \times 10^{-7} \, mol \, dm^{-3}$ at $25 \, ^{\circ}C$, the solution is called a ___.
In 1909, Soren P. Sorenson proposed the ___ scale.
The pH scale ranges from ___ that is used to measure the acidity or alkalinity of a solution.
The negative logarithm to base 10 of the molar concentration of ___ is used to calculate the pH.
The negative logarithm to base 10 of the molar concentration of ___ is defined as pOH.
The relation between the concentrations of hydrogen and hydroxide ions expressed as ___ is easily derived from the $K_{w}$ expression.
At $298 \, K$, the value of $pH + pOH$ is ___.
Acids have pH values of ___.
Alkalis have pH values of ___.
For acids, the smaller the pH value, the ___ the acid is.
Neutral solutions have a pH value of ___.
The solutions of different pH values develop their respective different colours with the use of a ___.
Strong acids are ___ that completely ionise in water.
Hydrochloric acid ($HCl$), nitric acid ($HNO_{3}$), and sulphuric acid ($H_{2}SO_{4}$) are examples of ___.
Weak acids are weak electrolytes that partially ionise only to a ___ in water.
The number of $H^{+}$ ions that one molecule of acid can produce is the ___.
The basicity of hydrochloric acid is ___ because each molecule can give only one $H^{+}$ ion.
The basicity of sulphuric acid is ___.
Acids that donate a maximum of one proton are called ___ acids.
Acids that donate a maximum of two protons are called ___ acids.
Acids that donate a maximum of three protons are called ___ acids.
In common usage, the word ___ and 'base' are interchangeable.
Hydroxides of alkali metals and certain alkaline earth metals are ___.
Ammonia, aniline, methylamine, and pyridine are examples of ___.
The ___ of a base is measured by the number of molecules of a monoprotic acid that will neutralise one molecule of the base.
Calcium hydroxide ($Ca(OH)_{2}$) has an acidity of ___.
The relative strengths of acids may be determined by measuring the ___.
The larger the $K_{a}$ value of an acid, the greater its dissociation and the ___ the acid.
The dissociation constant of a stronger acid is ___ than that of a weaker acid.
The dissociation constant of a weak acid is sometimes expressed on a logarithmic scale denoted by the relation ___.
Small $K_{a}$ values correspond to ___ $pK_{a}$ values.
The relative strengths of bases can be determined by measuring their ___.
The higher the $K_{b}$ value of a base, the higher the concentrations of ___ will be.
Water is ___ in the equilibrium expression for $K_{b}$ because its concentration does not change significantly.
The dissociation constant for a base is denoted by the relation ___.
Weak acids are weak electrolytes that ___ only to a limited extent in water.
The strength of an acid is related to its ___.
When solutions of an acid and a base are mixed, an ___ occurs.
Salts are ___ that completely dissociate into ions in water.
The term ___ describes the reaction of an anion or a cation of a salt, or both, with water.
Salt hydrolysis usually affects the ___ of a solution.
Solutions of salts of strong bases and strong acids are ___ ($pH = 7$).
The cation $Na^{+}$ and the anion $Cl^{-}$ of $NaCl$ do not ___.
Sodium ethanoate ($CH_{3}COONa$) is the salt of the ___ and the weak acid ethanoic acid.
The anion $CH_{3}COO^{-}$ is a ___ conjugate base of the weak acid $CH_{3}COOH$.
The solution of sodium ethanoate becomes ___ ($pH > 7$).
Ammonium chloride ($NH_{4}Cl$) is the salt of the ___ and the strong acid hydrochloric acid.
The cation $NH_{4}^{+}$ of $NH_{4}Cl$ is a ___ conjugate acid of the weak base $NH_{4}OH$.
The solution of ammonium chloride becomes ___ ($pH < 7$).
For a salt derived from a weak acid and a weak base, both the ___ hydrolyse.
If $K_{b}$ for the anion is greater than $K_{a}$ for the cation, the solution must be ___.
If $K_{b}$ for the anion is smaller than $K_{a}$ for the cation, the solution will be ___.
The pH of blood is maintained by ___ in most cases.
A ___ is a solution that resists changes in pH as a result of dilution or small addition of acids or bases.
The two common kinds of buffer solutions are solutions containing a weak acid and its salt and a ___ and its salt.
A typical buffer solution for acidic values of pH contains a ___.
In an acidic buffer, sodium ethanoate provides a large ___ of ethanoate ions.
In an acidic buffer, ethanoic acid provides a large potential reserve of ___.
A buffer solution of pH 7-11 can be prepared with a ___.
A mixture of ammonium hydroxide and ___ is used for basic buffer solutions.
In a basic buffer, ammonium chloride provides a large source of ___.
In a basic buffer, ammonium hydroxide provides a large potential source of ___.
The odour of fish is due to the presence of ___.
Lemon juice or vinegar on cooked fish reduces the odour through a ___.
Body lotion is buffered to a ___ to relieve rashes and inhibit pathogen growth.
Vitamin C is also known as ___.
___ is the result of the acid attacking the calcium compound that makes up the tooth's enamel.
